title: Naked Single
description: When a cell has only ONE candidate left, that must be the answer!
concept: If all numbers 1-9 except one have been eliminated from a cell, the remaining number is the only possibility. This is the simplest Sudoku technique.

steps.text: Welcome to your first Sudoku lesson! 🎉 A 'candidate' is a number that could possibly go in a cell. Let's find a cell with only one candidate left.
Look at the diagonal — but actually, let's focus on a specific cell. See cell at row 1, column 2 (near the top).
This highlighted cell is in row 1. Row 1 already has 4, 9, 5, 7. That leaves 1, 2, 3, 6, 8 as candidates.
The cell is also in column 2. Column 2 has 1, 7, 3, 9, 5 already placed. And in the same 3×3 box, we can see more numbers.
After eliminating all numbers that appear in the same row, column, and box — only ONE number is left! This is called a 'Naked Single'.
Can you figure out what number goes here? Look at the pencil marks (candidates) in the cell.
The answer is 6! 🎯 When a cell has only one candidate, it's called a Naked Single. This is the most basic solving technique.

title: Hidden Single
description: When a number can only go in ONE place within a row, column, or box — it must go there!
concept: Even if a cell has multiple candidates, if a specific number can only go in one cell within a group (row/col/box), it must go there. The number is 'hidden' among other candidates.

steps.text: Great job earning your Yellow Belt! 🌟 Now let's learn 'Hidden Single'. Sometimes a cell has multiple candidates, but a specific number can ONLY go in one place in a group.
Look at row 1. We need to place all numbers 1-9. Which numbers are missing?
Row 1 has 5 and 3. We need: 1, 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9. Each empty cell has multiple candidates, but let's check where 9 can go.
Look at the pencil marks for this cell. Check if 9 can go anywhere else in row 1...
After checking all empty cells in row 1, the number 9 can only go in ONE specific cell. Even though that cell has other candidates too, 9 is 'hidden' — it must go here!
Which cell in row 1 is the only place where 9 can go? Look at the candidates carefully!
The number 9 can only go in this cell! 🎯 Even though the cell had other candidates, 9 was 'hidden' as the only option in the row. This is a Hidden Single!

title: Naked Pair
description: When two cells in the same group have the same two candidates, those numbers can be eliminated from other cells in the group.
concept: If two cells in a row/column/box can only contain the same two numbers, then those two numbers MUST occupy those two cells. We can remove them from all other cells in the group.

steps.text: Orange Belt time! 🧡 The Naked Pair technique is powerful. If TWO cells share the exact same TWO candidates, we can eliminate those numbers from other cells in the group.
Imagine a row where two empty cells both have only candidates {3, 7}. Since one must be 3 and the other must be 7, no other cell in that row can be 3 or 7!
Look at row 1 — these two highlighted cells. Check their pencil marks. They both share the same two candidates!
These two cells form a 'Naked Pair'. If cell A is {3,6} and cell B is also {3,6}, then 3 and 6 are locked into these two positions.
Now look at the other cells in this row. The values from the naked pair can be eliminated as candidates from all of these cells!
What values does the naked pair eliminate from the other cells? Think about which two numbers are shared.
By identifying the Naked Pair {3, 6}, we can remove 3 and 6 as candidates from all other cells in the row! 🎯 This narrows down possibilities everywhere else.
